revision.text:
[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' (鴨川 or 賀茂川 ''Kamogawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is 31km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from Mount Sajiki in the north of [[Kyoto|Kyoto City]], and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for the residents and tourists.
== History ==
It was the east limits of [[Heiankyo]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe the river was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa of Japan|Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable matters: fighting monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River.
{{Japan-geo-stub}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]
[[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' (鴨川 or 賀茂川 ''Kamogawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from Mount Sajiki in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for the residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
== History ==
It was the east limits of [[Heiankyo]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe the river was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa of Japan|Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: fighting monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River.
The combat between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]].
The merchant [[Suminokura Ryoi|Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed Takase Canal (高瀬川 ''Takasegawa'') on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable Kamo River.
Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the east end of the [[Tokaido]] during the [[Edo period]].
{{Japan-geo-stub}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]
[[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

It was the east limits of [[Heiankyo]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa of Japan|Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: armed monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryoi|Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase Canal (高瀬川 ''Takasegawa'') on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tokaido]] during the [[Edo period]].
{{Japan-geo-stub}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]

revision.comment: I added some details about the current status of the river.
revision.text:
[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' (鴨川 or 賀茂川 ''Kamogawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from Mount Sajiki in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for the residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
Throughout Kyoto city, there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relativly low--significantly less than one meter in most places--during the rainy season, these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
Demachiyanagi, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the Takano River, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
== History ==
It was the east limits of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: armed monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryoi|Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase Canal (高瀬川 ''Takasegawa'') on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tokaido|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the purity of the water was a crucial source of drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a major role in Fushimi sake--famous in Japan--and for fabric dying.
{{Kyoto-geo-stub}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]
[[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' (鴨川 or 賀茂川 ''Kamogawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31 km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from Mount Sajiki in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for the residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
Throughout Kyoto city, there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relativly low--significantly less than one meter in most places--during the rainy season, these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|Takano River|高野川|Takanogawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The source of the Kamo River is Sajikigatake Mountain in the Northern ward of Kyoto city. After running through the mountain town of Kumogahata, it enters the Kyoto basin at Kamigamo in the North ward. Running South-Southeast between Kamigamo Shrine and Shimogamo Shrine, it is joined by the Takano River right at the Kamo Bridge and then continues due South through Kyoto City’s Central ward. In the vicinity of the Shijo Bridge, it shifts again towards the Southwest and runs until Kamitoba in the southern Fushimi ward where it flows into the Katsura River. When running through the Nakagyo ward the Kamo River splits West into the Takase River which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics==
Officially the river is called Kamogawa, using the kanji compound鴨川. However, north of the point where it is met by the Takano River, it is generally known by the same name, but different kanji compound 賀茂川 (and less frequently加茂川). Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village, and then north of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
It was the east limits of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: armed monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryoi|Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the {{nihongo|Takase Canal|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tokaido|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the purity of the water was a crucial source of drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
{{Kyoto-geo-stub}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' (鴨川 or 賀茂川 ''Kamogawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31 km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from Mount Sajiki in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for the residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
Throughout Kyoto city, there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relativly low--significantly less than one meter in most places--during the rainy season, these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|Takano River|高野川|Takanogawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The source of the Kamo River is Sajikigatake Mountain in the Northern ward of Kyoto city. After running through the mountain town of Kumogahata, it enters the Kyoto basin at Kamigamo in the North ward. Running South-Southeast between Kamigamo Shrine and Shimogamo Shrine, it is joined by the Takano River right at the Kamo Bridge (at Imadegawa Street) and then continues due South through Kyoto City’s Nakagyo (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijo Bridge, flanking the famous Gion area, it shifts again towards the Southwest and runs until Kamitoba in the southern Fushimi ward where it flows into the Katsura River. When running through the Nakagyo ward, the Kamo River splits West into the Takase River which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics==
Officially the river is called Kamogawa, using the kanji compound鴨川. However, north of the point where it is met by the Takano River, it is generally known by the same name, but different kanji compound 賀茂川 (and less frequently加茂川). Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village, and then north of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
It was the east limits of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: armed monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryoi|Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the {{nihongo|Takase Canal|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tokaido|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the purity of the water was a crucial source of drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
{{Kyoto-geo-stub}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]
[[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tokaido|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the purity of the water was a crucial source of drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' (鴨川 or 賀茂川 ''Kamogawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31 km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from Mount Sajikigatake in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for the residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
Throughout Kyoto city, there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relatively low--significantly less than one meter in most places--during the rainy season, these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|Takano River|高野川|Takanogawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The source of the Kamo River is Sajikigatake Mountain in the Northern ward of Kyoto city. After running through the mountain town of Kumogahata, it enters the Kyoto basin at Kamigamo in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita]] (North) ward. Running South-Southeast between Kamigamo Shrine and Shimogamo Shrine, it is joined by the Takano River right at the Kamo Bridge (at Imadegawa Street) and then continues due South through Kyoto City’s [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyo]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijo Bridge, flanking the famous Gion area, it shifts again towards the Southwest and runs until Kamitoba in the southern Fushimi ward where it flows into the Katsura River. When running through the Nakagyo ward, the Kamo River splits West into the {{nihongo|Takase River|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics==
Officially the river is called Kamogawa, using the kanji compound 鴨川 - ''kamo'' meaning wild duck, and ''gawa'' meaning river. However, north of Imadegawa Street (the point where it is met by the Takano River) the kamogawa is generally written using a different kanji compound, 賀茂川 (and less frequently 加茂川). Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社). [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp] Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
It was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: armed monks, dice, and water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryoi|Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge over the river is a famous legend laid in the latest [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tokaido|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the purity of the water was a crucial source of drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
[[Category:Rivers of Japan]]
{{Kyoto-geo-stub}}
[[de:Kamo (Fluss)]] [[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 鴨川 or 賀茂川, ''Kamo-gawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31 km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from [[Mount Sajikigatake]] in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
In Kyoto there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relatively low—significantly less than one meter in most places—during the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|Takano River|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The source of the Kamo River is Sajikigatake Mountain in the Northern ward of Kyoto city. After running through the mountain town of Kumogahata, it enters the Kyoto basin at Kamigamo in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita]] (North) ward. Running south-south-east between Kamigamo Shrine and Shimogamo Shrine, it is joined by the Takano River at the Kamo Bridge (at Imadegawa Street) and then continues due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyo]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijo Bridge, flanking the famous Gion area, it shifts again towards the south-west and runs until Kamitoba in the southern Fushimi ward where it flows into the Katsura River. When running through the Nakagyo ward, the Kamo River splits west into the {{nihongo|Takase River|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', and is generally written using the kanji compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, north of Imadegawa Street (the point where it is met by the Takano River) ''Kamo-gawa'' is generally written using a different kanji compound, 賀茂川 (and less frequently 加茂川). <!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

revision.comment: [[WP:UNDO|Undid]] revision 280916804 by [[Special:Contributions/Nihonjoe|Nihonjoe]] ([[User talk:Nihonjoe|talk]]) Yurihonjo is far away from Kyoto
revision.text:
[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' ([[Japanese language|Japanese]]: 鴨川 or 賀茂川, ''Kamo-gawa'') is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. It is 31 km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from [[Mount Sajikigatake]] in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
In Kyoto there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relatively low—significantly less than one meter in most places—during the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|Takano River|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The source of the Kamo River is Sajikigatake Mountain in the Northern ward of Kyoto city. After running through the mountain town of Kumogahata, it enters the Kyoto basin at Kamigamo in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita]] (North) ward. Running south-south-east between Kamigamo Shrine and Shimogamo Shrine, it is joined by the Takano River at the Kamo Bridge (at Imadegawa Street) and then continues due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyo]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijo Bridge, flanking the famous Gion area, it shifts again towards the south-west and runs until Kamitoba in the southern Fushimi ward where it flows into the Katsura River. When running through the Nakagyo ward, the Kamo River splits west into the {{nihongo|Takase River|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', and is generally written using the kanji compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, north of Imadegawa Street (the point where it is met by the Takano River) ''Kamo-gawa'' is generally written using a different kanji compound, 賀茂川 (and less frequently 加茂川). <!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
==External links== {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=inline,title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Japan]]
{{Kyoto-geo-stub}}
[[de:Kamo (Fluss)]] [[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, this name may be written in [[kanji]] as either {{nihongo||鴨川 or 賀茂川}}. The Kamo River is 31 km long and is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from [[Mount Sajikigatake]] in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
In Kyoto there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relatively low—significantly less than one meter in most places—during the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The source of the Kamo River is Sajikigatake Mountain in the Northern ward of Kyoto city. After running through the mountain town of Kumogahata, it enters the Kyoto basin at Kamigamo in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|Kita]] (North) ward. Running south-south-east between Kamigamo Shrine and Shimogamo Shrine, it is joined by the Takano River at the Kamo Bridge (at Imadegawa Street) and then continues due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyo]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijo Bridge, flanking the famous Gion area, it shifts again towards the south-west and runs until Kamitoba in the southern Fushimi ward where it flows into the Katsura River. When running through the Nakagyo ward, the Kamo River splits west into the {{nihongo|Takase River|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', and is generally written using the kanji compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, north of Imadegawa Street (the point where it is met by the Takano River) ''Kamo-gawa'' is generally written using a different kanji compound, 賀茂川 (and less frequently 加茂川). <!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

When running through the Nakagyo ward, the Kamo River splits west into the {{nihongo|Takase River|高瀬川|Takasegawa}} which runs southward parallel to it.
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', and is generally written using the kanji compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, north of Imadegawa Street (the point where it is met by the Takano River) ''Kamo-gawa'' is generally written using a different kanji compound, 賀茂川 (and less frequently 加茂川). <!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->Otherwise, from the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> It is a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].
It rises from [[Mount Sajikigatake]] in the north of Kyoto, and goes through the center of the city and then meets the [[Katsura River]] at [[Fushimi, Kyoto|Fushimi]]. The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river.
In Kyoto there are pathways on which one can walk along the river. Though the water level is usually relatively low—significantly less than one meter in most places—during the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], these pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.

The Katsura River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the Kyoto Basin from the city area called Kamigamo in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as Demachi in the Kamigyō or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the Takano River which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of the Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>

At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of the Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記）. In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the abbreviated edition of the ''[[Nihonki]]'' （日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. However, now the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten" ibid.</ref>
<!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia, entry "Kamogawa'' (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==

The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記）. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).

Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia, entry "Kamogawa'' (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The Katsura River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the Kyoto Basin from the city area called Kamigamo in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as Demachi in the Kamigyō or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the Takano River which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>

The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記）. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo （賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo （賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>

The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia, entry "Kamogawa'' (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The Katsura River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the Kyoto Basin from the city area called Kamigamo in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as Demachi in the Kamigyō or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the Takano River which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about 1 km. north of Misono Bridge, and when the Heian Capitol was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten' (ibid)</ref>. According to historian Herbert E. Plutschow, "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記）. In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the abbreviated edition of the ''[[Nihonki]]'' （日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo （賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo （賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
<!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia, entry "Kamogawa'' (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
{{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==

In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>

The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>

Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo （賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo （賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>

Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. {{nihongo|Demachiyanagi|出町柳}}, in northern Kyoto where the Kamo River joins the {{nihongo|[[Takano River]]|高野川|Takano-gawa}}, is a popular spot for barbecues in the summer.
==Geographic Description==
The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the Kyoto Basin from the city area called Kamigamo in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as Demachi in the Kamigyō or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the Takano River which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about 1 km. north of Misono Bridge, and when the Heian Capital was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten' (ibid)</ref>. According to historian Herbert E. Plutschow, "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記）. In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the abbreviated edition of the ''[[Nihonki]]'' （日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo （賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo （賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
<!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia, entry "Kamogawa'' (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geographic Description==
The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref>

According to historian Herbert E. Plutschow, "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>

The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記）. In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the abbreviated edition of the ''[[Nihonki]]'' （日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo （賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo （賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
<!-- Presumably this is due to its proximity in that area to the [[Kamigamo Shrine]] (上賀茂神社) [http://www.kamigamojinja.jp]. < -- NO. Kamo is just a name of the area and a clan. -->From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).

Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo(賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto, entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geographic Description==
The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the Kyoto Basin from the city area called Kamigamo in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as Demachi in the Kamigyō or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the Takano River which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid).</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about 1 km. north of Misono Bridge, and when the Heian Capital was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian Herbert E. Plutschow, "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro kuni fudōki'' (山城国不土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo(賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Atagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).

The Kamo River was the eastern limit of [[Heiankyo|Heiankyō]], and had a significant meaning according to [[fengshui]]. Since the route of the river is highly linear, some people believe it was artificial, though no record of massive construction exists.
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].

revision.text:
[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately 23 km long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto, entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geographic Description==
The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of Mount Sajikigatake, around the boundary of Kumogahata village and Keihoku village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the Kyoto Basin from the city area called Kamigamo in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as Demachi in the Kamigyō or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the Takano River which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the Shirakawa River joins with it. At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid).</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about 1 km. north of Misono Bridge, and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian Herbert E. Plutschow, "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th c., the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

At its southern part, the Horikawa River and West Takase River join with it, and at Shimomukōjima-cho in the Shimotoba part of Fushimi Ward of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid).</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. north of Misono Bridge, and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian Herbert E. Plutschow, "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th c., the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
