[[Image:Kamogawa sakura.jpg|thumb|300px|right|The Kamo River and cherry blossoms]] The '''Kamo River''' is a river in [[Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. Called Kamo-''gawa'' in Japanese, it is approximately {{convert|23|km|0|abbr=on}} long and covers a land area of approximately 20.7 square kilometers.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto, entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1.</ref> The riverbanks are popular walks for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geographic Description==
The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto City. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] or "Upper Kyoto" ward, joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto City's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] (Central) ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid).</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>

The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>

These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].

Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan). The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara. ==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川."Kyoto Daijiten", ibid. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th c., the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[ar:نهر كامو]] [[de:Kamo (Fluss)]] [[et:Kamo jõgi]] [[ko:가모가와 강]] [[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

{{Infobox River | river_name = Kamo River
鴨川 | image_name = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | origin = | mouth = [[Yodo River]] | basin_countries = [[Japan]] | length_km = 31 | elevation_m =896 | mouth_elevation_m = | discharge_m3/s = | watershed_km2 = 210 | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways on which one can walk along the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[tsuyu|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches. ==Geographic Description== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid). There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid) According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th c., the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[ar:نهر كامو]] [[de:Kamo (Fluss)]] [[et:Kamo jõgi]] [[ko:가모가와 강]] [[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]] [[ru:Камо (река, Япония)]]

{{Infobox River | river_name = Kamo River
鴨川 | image_name = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | origin = | mouth = [[Yodo River]] | basin_countries = [[Japan]] | length_km = 31 | elevation_m =896 | mouth_elevation_m = | discharge_m3/s = | watershed_km2 = 210 | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches. ==Geographic Description== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid). There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid) According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan). The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara. ==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川."Kyoto Daijiten", ibid. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th c., the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[ar:نهر كامو]] [[de:Kamo (Fluss)]] [[et:Kamo jõgi]] [[ko:가모가와 강]] [[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]] [[ru:Камо (приток Ёдо)]]

revision.comment: Added hatnote pointing to Kamogawa page, where I have disambiguated a number of other Kamo rivers thruout Japan.

Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[ar:نهر كامو]] [[de:Kamo (Fluss)]] [[et:Kamo jõgi]] [[ko:가모가와 강]] [[la:Camovius (aquae Iodovicae)]] [[ja:鴨川 (淀川水系)]] [[ru:Камо (приток Ёдо)]] [[zh:鴨川 (淀川水系)]]

revision.text:
{{About|the river through Kyoto|dozens of other rivers and streams by this name|Kamogawa (disambiguation){{!}}Kamogawa}} {{Infobox River | river_name = Kamo River
鴨川 | image_name = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | origin = | mouth = [[Yodo River]] | basin_countries = [[Japan]] | length_km = 31 | elevation_m =896 | mouth_elevation_m = | discharge_m3/s = | watershed_km2 = 210 | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches. ==Geographic Description== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid). There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid) According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan). The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara. ==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川."Kyoto Daijiten", ibid. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th c., the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]]

There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches. ==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1. There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid) According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan). The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara. ==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川."Kyoto Daijiten", ibid. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) Floods often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the Takase River on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyoyuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]]

The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan). The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara. ==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川."Kyoto Daijiten", ibid. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) [[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in [[Kyo Yuzen]] dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP-26_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]]

{{About|the river through Kyoto|dozens of other rivers and streams by this name|Kamogawa (disambiguation){{!}}Kamogawa}} {{Infobox River | river_name = Kamo River
鴨川 | image_name = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | origin = | mouth = [[Yodo River]] | basin_countries = [[Japan]] | length_km = 31 | elevation_m =896 | mouth_elevation_m = | discharge_m3/s = | watershed_km2 = 210 | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }} The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches. ==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN4-473-00885-1. There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid) According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan). The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara. ==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川."Kyoto Daijiten", ibid. The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid. From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川). == History == When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983) [[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream. The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]]. In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto. ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commonscat-inline|Kamo River}} {{coord|34.931416|135.737236|format=dms|display=title|type:river_region:JP-26_scale:100000}} [[Category:Rivers of Kyoto Prefecture]]

The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. ISBN 4-473-00885-1.</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. ISBN 0-87011-904-4 (USA); ISBN 4-7700-1404-X (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
[[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
[[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.

{{About|the river through Kyoto|dozens of other rivers and streams by this name|Kamogawa (disambiguation){{!}}Kamogawa}} {{Infobox river | name = Kamo River | name_native = 鴨川 | image = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | image_caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | source1_location = | mouth_location = [[Yodo River]] | basin_countries = [[Japan]] | length = {{convert|31|km|mi|abbr=on}} | source1_elevation = {{convert|896|m|ft|abbr=on}} | mouth_elevation = | discharge1_avg = | basin_size = {{convert|210|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }}
The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. {{ISBN|4-473-00885-1}}.</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. {{ISBN|0-87011-904-4}} (USA); {{ISBN|4-7700-1404-X}} (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>

The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].

The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
[[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
The river is also the source of the stone that is a glaze ingredient used in traditional Japanese raku pottery. <ref> https://www.2000cranes.com/Raku.html </ref>

{{About|the river through Kyoto|dozens of other rivers and streams by this name|Kamogawa (disambiguation){{!}}Kamogawa}} {{Infobox river | name = Kamo River | name_native = 鴨川 | image = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | image_caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | source1_location = | mouth_location = [[Yodo River]] | subdivision_type1 = Country | subdivision_name1 = [[Japan]] | length = {{convert|31|km|mi|abbr=on}} | source1_elevation = {{convert|896|m|ft|abbr=on}} | mouth_elevation = | discharge1_avg = | basin_size = {{convert|210|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }}
The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. {{ISBN|4-473-00885-1}}.</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=on}}. North of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. {{ISBN|0-87011-904-4}} (USA); {{ISBN|4-7700-1404-X}} (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
[[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
The river is also the source of the stone that is a glaze ingredient used in traditional Japanese raku pottery. <ref> https://www.2000cranes.com/Raku.html </ref>

Tankosha 1984. {{ISBN|4-473-00885-1}}.</ref>

Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. {{ISBN|0-87011-904-4}} (USA); {{ISBN|4-7700-1404-X}} (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
[[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. Sanjō Bridge was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
The river is also the source of the stone that is a glaze ingredient used in traditional Japanese raku pottery. <ref> https://www.2000cranes.com/Raku.html </ref>
The Kamo River is also the birthplace of [[Kabuki]]. In 1603 [[Izumo no Okuni]] formed a troupe of female dancers and began performing on a makeshift stage, on the dry bed of the river<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/biography/Okuni|title=Okuni {{!}} Kabuki dancer|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en|access-date=2019-05-05}}</ref>.

{{About|the river through Kyoto|dozens of other rivers and streams by this name|Kamogawa (disambiguation){{!}}Kamogawa}} {{Infobox river | name = Kamo River | name_native = 鴨川 | image = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | image_caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | source1_location = | mouth_location = [[Yodo River]] | subdivision_type1 = Country | subdivision_name1 = [[Japan]] | length = {{convert|31|km|mi|abbr=on}} | source1_elevation = {{convert|896|m|ft|abbr=on}} | mouth_elevation = | discharge1_avg = | basin_size = {{convert|210|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }}
[[File:Noryo yuka of a Korean restaurant by Wolfiewolf in Pontocho, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Hiroshige55 kyoto.jpg|thumb|[[Hiroshige]]]] The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. {{ISBN|4-473-00885-1}}.</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=off}} north of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's Imadegawa Street)."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. {{ISBN|0-87011-904-4}} (USA); {{ISBN|4-7700-1404-X}} (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. {{About|the river through Kyoto|dozens of other rivers and streams by this name|Kamogawa (disambiguation){{!}}Kamogawa}} {{Infobox river | name = Kamo River | name_native = 鴨川 | image = Kamogawa sakura.jpg | image_size = | image_caption = Cherry blossoms along the Kamo River | source1_location = | mouth_location = [[Yodo River]] | subdivision_type1 = Country | subdivision_name1 = [[Japan]] | length = {{convert|31|km|mi|abbr=on}} | source1_elevation = | mouth_elevation = | discharge1_avg = | basin_size = {{convert|210|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} | river_system = [[Yodo River]] }}
[[File:Noryo yuka of a Korean restaurant by Wolfiewolf in Pontocho, Kyoto.jpg|thumb|]] [[File:Hiroshige55 kyoto.jpg|thumb|[[Hiroshige]]]] The {{nihongo|'''Kamo River'''|鴨川| Kamo-gawa| duck river – see [[#Onomastics|onomastics]]}} is located in [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The riverbanks are popular walking spots for residents and tourists. In summer, restaurants open balconies looking out to the river. There are pathways running alongside the river on which one can walk along the river, and some stepping stones that cross the river. The water level of the river is usually relatively low; less than one meter in most places. During the [[East Asian rainy season|rainy season]], however, the pathways sometimes flood in their lower stretches.
==Geography== The Kamo River has its source in the mountains in the area of [[Mount Sajikigatake]], around the boundary of [[Kumogahata]] village and [[Keihoku]] village in the [[Kita-ku, Kyoto|northern ward]] of Kyoto. Flowing into the [[Kyoto Basin]] from the city area called [[Kamigamo]] in the same northern ward of the city, from there it bends south-east and, around the spot known as [[Demachi]] in the [[Kamigyō-ku|Kamigyō]] (or "Upper Kyoto" ward), joins with the [[Takano River]] which flows down from the northeastern direction, and there changes direction to due south through Kyoto's [[Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto|Nakagyō]] ("Central") ward. In the vicinity of the Shijō Bridge at Shijō Street in the center of downtown Kyoto, the [[Shirakawa River]] joins with it. At its southern part, the [[Horikawa River]] and [[West Takase River]] join with it, and at [[Shimomukōjima-cho]] in the [[Shimotoba]] part of [[Fushimi Ward]] of Kyoto City, it joins with the [[Katsura River]], to become a tributary of the [[Yodo River]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (Japanese encyclopedia of Kyoto), entry "Kamogawa" (鴨川; which refers from 賀茂川). Tankosha 1984. {{ISBN|4-473-00885-1}}.</ref>
There is a theory that in former times the main stream of the Kamo River was along the Horikawa river about {{convert|1|km|0|abbr=off}} north of [[Misono Bridge]], and when the [[Heian-kyō|Heian Capital]] (now Kyoto) was established, the river was diverted to its present route.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'' (ibid)</ref> According to historian [[Herbert E. Plutschow]], "To allow a river to flow through and thereby divide a capital would have symbolized potential disunity of the nation. One of the first tasks, therefore, in laying out the new capital was to divert the rivers. The Kamo River once flowed through what is now Horikawa Street and met the Takano River south of their present confluence. Thus, large-scale works were required to prepare the land for the capital. The city was laid with its northernmost boundary at the present confluence of the Kamo and Takano rivers (just south of today's [[Imadegawa Street]])."<ref>''Introducing Kyoto'' (Kodansha International Ltd.), with text by Herbert E. Plutschow and Foreword by Donald Keene, p. 34. {{ISBN|0-87011-904-4}} (USA); {{ISBN|4-7700-1404-X}} (Japan).</ref>
The riverbanks where the Kamo River and Takano River join are known as the Tadasu River Banks (Jp., Tadasu-gawara 糺河原). At the triangular area of land here, there is the "River Confluence" shrine of [[Shimogamo Shrine]], which leads into the forested area, [[Tadasu no Mori|Tadasu-no-mori]].<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', entry for Tadasu-gawara.</ref>
==Onomastics== In Japanese the river is called ''Kamo-gawa'', officially written using the [[kanji]] compound 鴨川. The first kanji means "wild duck" and is read ''kamo'', and the second kanji means "river" and is read ''gawa''. However, other kanji applied to this name are 賀茂川 or 加茂川. The first appearance in historical documents of the kanji 賀茂川 is in the ''Yamashiro no kuni fudoki'' (山城国風土記). In an entry dated the 19th day of the 6th month of 815 in the history ''Nihonkiryaku'' (日本記略), it is referred to as 鴨川. Now, the river north of where it joins with the Takano River is usually distinguished as the 加茂川 when written in kanji, and the river south of there is distinguished as the 鴨川.<ref>"Kyoto Daijiten", ibid.</ref>
The geographical area called Kamigamo, around the mouth of the valley leading into the Kyoto Basin, became the home grounds of the Kamo (賀茂) clan in ancient times. From this, there arose the regional name Otagi Region Kamo Block (愛宕郡賀茂郷), and consequently the geographical name Kamo (賀茂) set down roots. The river name took after this geographical name.<ref>''Kyoto Daijiten'', ibid.</ref>
From the Deai Bridge until the Iwaya Bridge north of Kyoto city it is called Kumogahatagawa (雲が畑川) as it passes through the Kumogahata village. North of the Iwaya Bridge until its source the Kamo River is known as Ojitanigawa (祖父谷川).
== History ==
When the palace at the new Heian Capital (now Kyoto) was constructed at the end of the 8th century, the river's course was altered to flow east of the palace.<ref>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan, entry for "Kamogawa." (Kodansha Ltd., 1983)</ref>
[[Flood]]s often threatened the ancient capital. [[Emperor Shirakawa]] recited his three unmanageable things: [[Sōhei]] (armed monks of [[Enryaku-ji]]), dice, and the water of the Kamo River. These days, however, the riverbanks are reinforced with concrete and have improved drainage systems. The merchant [[Suminokura Ryōi]] constructed the [[Takase River]] on a parallel with the Kamo River in early 17th century. Transportation was done on the canal instead of the unstable mainstream.
The encounter between [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]] and [[Benkei]] at Gojō Bridge (not the present one, but presumably Matsubara Bridge) over the river is a famous legend set in the late [[Heian period]]. [[Sanjō Ōhashi | Sanjō Bridge]] was regarded as the west end of the [[Tōkaidō (road)|Tōkaidō]] during the [[Edo period]].
In the past, the river was a crucial source of relatively pure drinking water for Kyoto residents. It also played a role in Kyo-Yuzen dyeing, a famous craft of Kyoto.
The river is also the source of the stone that is a glaze ingredient used in traditional Japanese raku pottery.<ref>https://www.2000cranes.com/Raku.html</ref>
The Kamo River is also the birthplace of [[Kabuki]]. In 1603 [[Izumo no Okuni]] formed a troupe of female dancers and began performing on a makeshift stage, on the dry bed of the river.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.britannica.com/biography/Okuni|title=Okuni {{!}} Kabuki dancer|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en|access-date=2019-05-05}}</ref>
